source_metadata.title_raw: Sue Gallagher - A-19-045 - March 28, 2019 - Santa Rosa

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 28, 2019


Sue Gallagher
City Attorney
City Attorney's Office
1100 Santa Rosa Avenue, Room 8
Santa Rosa, CA 95404

Re:     Your Request for Advice
        Our File No. A-19-045

Dear Ms. Gallagher:

        This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Santa Rosa City Councilmember
Jack Tibbetts regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

        Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090.

        Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice.

QUESTION

        May Councilmember Tibbetts participate in a decision regarding land use entitlements for a
new hotel where he is the Executive Director of a local non-profit organization and a donor to the
non-profit currently opposes the hotel project?

CONCLUSION

        Yes. There are no facts to indicate that his non-profit employer will receive a measurable
financial benefit or loss as a result of the decision. Therefore, he does not have a reasonably
foreseeable and material financial interest in the decision and he may participate.


_________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A -19-045
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

Councilmember Tibbetts is employed by St. Vincent de Paul of Sonoma County ("St. Vincent") as its paid Executive Director. St. Vincent is a non-profit organization that seeks to end homelessness in Santa Rosa and surrounding areas. Its website identifies it as "a nonprofit spiritually-based organization of individuals seeking to alleviate poverty and hunger in Sonoma County." St. Vincent is headquered in the nearby town of Rohnert Park. Its services include operation of the Community Kitchen (formerly known as the Free Dining Room) located in Santa Rosa, which serves free hot meals to anyone in need. The Community Kitchen is open 365 days a year and serves all who enter. St. Vincent also operates a thrift store and job training program in Rohnert Park, and has an associated volunteer team that travels throughout the County, providing home visits, social service referrals, and a food pantry.

St. Vincent's operations are funded primarily through grants and donations. Last year, St. Vincent received over 800 separate donations, and it has a donor pool (current and previous donors) of over 2000 individuals and entities. St. Vincent's total annual budget is approximately $1.3 million. As Executive Director, Councilmember Tibbetts receives a flat salary. He does not receive any commission or incentive pay. His contract states nothing about raising funds for the organization and does not link the organization's fund-raising to his employment or any opportunity for increased payor benefits. He is an at-will employee.

Proposed Hotel Project and Community Opposition

Marriott Residence Inn ("Marriott") has proposed a new 114 room hotel in northeast Santa Rosa. Last December, the Santa Rosa Planning Commission rejected the proposal on a 3-3-1 vote. In accordance with procedures set forth in the Santa Rosa City Code, Marriott has appealed the decision to the City Council. That appeal is now pending. As both applicant and appellant, Marriott is the sole named party to the proceeding. The project, however, has been controversial and a number of community groups and neighbors have voiced, and continue to voice, their support for or opposition to the project.

Among the groups in vocal opposition to the project is Providence St. Joseph Health System ("SJH"), the local branch of a large non-profit health care system serving the Western United States. In association with its two local hospitals, SJH operates a cancer treatment center across the street from the site of the proposed hotel. Representatives of SJH have filed letters and spoken against the project as currently designed, due to its negative impact on views from the cancer center's infusion room. (SJH does not object to the hotel per se, but rather to its current proposed design.) You provide a copy of SJH's most recent opposition letter, which notes that SJH hopes to reach a compromise on the issue, but that it is of great importance to them.

Donations from SJH to St. Vincent

Under its umbrella of services, SJH participates in the SJH Community Partnership Fund, through which it provides, as stated on the Fund's website, "grants to organizations working to improve access to quality health care, food, shelter and other aspects of social well-being for the most vulnerable in our communities. Current grant-funding priorities include community building, emergency food and shelter, and disaster relief."

File No. A -19-045
Page No. 3

The SJH Community Partnership Fund also considers specific community needs. Every
three years, each of the Fund's local hospitals conducts a community health needs assessment and
develops strategies to identify and respond to unmet health-related needs in its region. In Sonoma
County, those efforts are administered by the St. Joseph Health Sonoma Community Benefit
Committee ("Committee") formed in 2007 by the two local SJH hospitals. Currently, the
Committee is comprised of five members of Board of Trustees of the Santa Rosa Memorial
Hospital, three members of the Board of Trustees of the Petaluma Valley Hospital and seven
members of the community with knowledge of and experience in the areas of concern. Each year,
the Committee issues a request for proposals from non-profits working in the fields of the identified
funding priorities.

Each year, St. Vincent reviews the request for proposals and submits an application, if the
year's funding priorities align with St. Vincent's work. In each of the past two years, St. Vincent
has received a grant of approximately $50,000 for its Community Kitchen (for a total of
approximately $100,000 combined over the two years). The grants have been, and continue to be,
used solely to support the operations of the Community Kitchen, including Community Kitchen
staff salaries and materials. None of the funds are used for general administration of the
organization or for any of St. Vincent's other programs. The grants represent approximately 4.5%
of St. Vincent's overall annual budget and approximately 12.5% of the Community Kitchen's
annual budget.

ANALYSIS

Under Section 87100, a public official may not make, participate in making, or use his or
her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the meaning
of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on
one or more of the public official's interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).)

Section 87103 identifies interests from which a conflict of interest may arise and includes:

• A business entity interest, where the public official has a direct or
indirect investment worth $ 2,000 or more (Section 87103(a).); or in
which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds
any position of management (Section 87103(d).)

• An interest in a source of income, aggregating five hundred dollars
($500) or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.
(Section 87103(c).)

• An interest in any donor, or any intermediary or agent for a donor, of a
gift or gifts aggregating $ 500 or more in value provided to, received by,
or promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made. (Section 87103(e).

File No. A -19-045
Page No. 4

• A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances,
including those of his or her immediate family (Section 87103.)

The definition of "business entity" under Section 82005 excludes non-profit organizations.
Therefore, Councilmember Tibbetts, as the paid Executive Director of St. Vincent, has a source of
income interest in St. Vincent. He does not have a financial interest in SJH as a source of income,
because his income is not tied to the donation. The donation is specifically to support St. Vincent's
Community Kitchen and is not for overhead costs of St. Vincent. Nor does he have an interest in
SJH as a donor of a gift, under these facts. A "gift" is defined under Section 82028, pertinent to
these facts as:

(a) . . . any payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent
that consideration of equal or greater value is not received . . . .

(b) the term "gift" does not include:

. . .

(2) Gifts which are not used and which, within 30 days after receipt, are either
returned to the donor or delivered to a nonprofit entity exempt from taxation
under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code without being claimed as a
charitable contribution for tax purposes.

Thus, a donation to a non-profit organization does not create a financial interest in a donor,
so long as the official receives no personal benefit and the donation is made to the non-profit
organization.² According to the facts, SJH is making a donation to St. Vincent, not to
Councilmember Tibbetts, and as stated above, the SJH donation is not conferring a benefit on
Councilmember Tibbetts. Please note that when elected officials fundraise for charitable purposes,
they may have to file beshested payments reports.³

Foreseeability and Materiality

The standard for foreseeability varies depending on whether an interest is explicitly
involved in the decision at issue. A financial effect is presumed reasonably foreseeable where the
official's financial interest it is explicitly involved as a named party in, or subject of, the decision.⁴
(Regulation 18701(a).) For a financial interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision, the

² Note that the Commission has previously advised that a donor to a non-profit employer could be the source of
gifts or income to the official employed by the non-profit under certain circumstances. (See Shaw Advice Letter,
No. A-87-045, where donations to a non-profit constituted a significant portion of salary of the president of the non-
profit.)

³ Section 84224 requires elected officers to report payments made at their behest that would not otherwise be
reported as contributions or gifts and are not made principally for a personal purpose. (See Section 82004.5 defining
beshested payments and Regulation 18215.3, reporting.)

⁴ A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval,
denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, or if the
decision affects a real property interest described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6).

File No. A -19-045
Page No. 5

financial effect of the decision on an official's interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be
recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. If the financial result
cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it
is not reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b).) Councilmember Tibbett's financial interest is
not explicitly involved in the hotel land use decisions, and the latter standard applies.

St. Vincent as a Source of Income

We next examine whether the hotel land use decision will have a material effect upon
St. Vincent. Regulation 18702.3(a)(3) provides that a decision has a material effect on a nonprofit
as a source of income where the nonprofit will receive "a measurable financial benefit or loss." It
does not appear from these facts that a decision on the new hotel poses a realistic possibility that
St. Vincent will suffer a loss or benefit. Intervening events would be required for a financial impact
on St. Vincent relating to SJH's donations. Additionally, those intervening events would seem to
present extraordinary circumstances beyond the official's control. The grant funds are allocated
through the SJH Community Partnership Fund, and by the SJH Sonoma Community Benefit
Committee, the latter of which is comprised of five members of Board of Trustees of the Santa
Rosa Memorial Hospital, three members of the Board of Trustees of the Petaluma Valley Hospital
and seven members of the community with knowledge of and experience in the areas of concern.
Both organizations have a process in place to determine community needs and funding priorities.
Absent facts to the contrary, it appears unlikely that the SJH Community Partnership Fund or the
SJH Community Benefit Committee would deviate from its established protocols and identified
community needs as a result of the decision. Therefore, Councilmember Tibbetts does not have a
reasonably foreseeable and material financial interest in the hotel decision.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: L. Karen Harrison
Counsel, Legal Division

LKH:jgl

non-profit currently opposes the hotel project?

QUESTION: May Councilmember Tibbetts participate in a decision regarding land use entitlements for a 
new hotel where he is the Executive Director of a local non-profit organization and a donor to the 
non-profit currently opposes the hotel project?

CONCLUSION: Yes. There are no facts to indicate that his non-profit employer will receive a measurable 
financial benefit or loss as a result of the decision. Therefore, he does not have a reasonably 
foreseeable and material financial interest in the decision and he may participate.
